meta.description: A laid-back dinner party with minimal disruption. Events are infrequent and low-key. Tests whether the system can maintain engagement at a slow burn.

votingMechanic.name: Standard Vote
votingMechanic.description: Everyone votes LEGIT or BULLSHIT. Majority rules.

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.dialogue: Wait, hold on—I literally just caught someone checking their phone like thirty seconds ago? That's... actually weak timing on my part. Everyone, vote however you want, this one's on me.
pat.internal_thought: Crap, I'm calling myself out on the first event and it's going to make me look either desperate or like I don't know what the hell I'm doing as host—either way this sets a weird tone before we even get momentum.

jade.dialogue: Ooh, Pat's already hunting! Yeah, I'm voting that legit — gotta support the hustle early, let's get everyone fired up!
jade.internal_thought: This is exactly what I'm here for — the game is LIVE and people are already paying attention to each other, this is gonna be chaotic and fun.

river.dialogue: Legit. Pretty sure I saw that.
river.internal_thought: Pat's playing it safe with an easy claim this early — smart move, and yeah, someone was definitely on their phone during setup.

diana.dialogue: Hold on—what exactly did you see? Because 'Phone Addict' requires someone actually checking their phone when they should be paying attention, and we're literally three minutes into setup. When was this supposed to happen?
diana.internal_thought: Pat's already trying to claim points on a mission that's barely had time to trigger, and I need to know if this is legitimate before I vote or I'm part of the problem.

sam.dialogue: Um... did Pat actually catch someone on their phone? I wasn't really paying attention to that.
sam.internal_thought: Okay so people are calling out stuff that happens—I need to watch for these things too, I guess? Pat seemed pretty confident about it but I have no idea if it actually happened because I was still reading the rules.

marcus.dialogue: Wait, who was even on their phone? I wasn't paying attention to Pat's phone, I was setting up my strategy. This feels like a reach.
marcus.internal_thought: It's early and we're in setup so I don't hate the interruption, but I'm already annoyed because I didn't see it happen and now I'm being asked to vote on something I didn't witness — that's sloppy claiming.

timeline.title: Celebrity Impression
timeline.description: First person to do a recognizable celebrity impression wins.

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.dialogue: Oh, this is perfect—okay everyone, who's got the best one? I'm ready to be entertained. Jade, you look like you're about to explode, go ahead!
pat.internal_thought: This is exactly what we need right now—low stakes, high energy, and everyone can participate. The group's still ramping up, and this gets people laughing and loose before we hit the heavier strategic stuff later.

river.internal_thought: Not doing an impression. That's not my thing and everyone knows it.

diana.dialogue: Wait—hold on. 'Recognizable' to who? And are we doing this simultaneously or is it turn-based? Because if everyone's just riffing at once, that's chaos, not a fair competition.
diana.internal_thought: Great, another vague mechanic right when we've barely started. Who judges 'recognizable'? This is exactly the kind of thing that gets messy.

sam.dialogue: Oh... um... I'm not really great at impressions. Maybe I'll just watch this one.
sam.internal_thought: Everyone's gonna do something funny and I'm gonna bomb it and look weird in front of these people I just met.

marcus.dialogue: Come on, we're five minutes in. Can we please just play the actual game instead of doing impressions every two minutes?
marcus.internal_thought: This is exactly the kind of random chaos that derails focus—I was just getting into the setup phase and now everyone's going to waste five minutes doing bad celebrity voices.

alex.dialogue: Oh, THIS is what I'm talking about! *immediately stands up* Okay, okay, picture this—
alex.internal_thought: First flash of the night and it's a soft one, easy points, and I get to set the tone for how ridiculous this is gonna get.
alex.submission_if_applicable: Alex does a brutally bad Morgan Freeman impression (deep voice, completely overdone) and commits HARD to it, maybe even staying in character for a few seconds after to milk the reaction.

votingMechanic.name: Pitch It
votingMechanic.description: The claimant gets 15 seconds to make their case. Then you vote.

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.dialogue: Okay, hold up—did I actually catch someone repeating a story? I wasn't even tracking that claim, I was focused on the impression thing. Let me think... no, I don't think that happened. I'm calling bullshit on my own claim here.
pat.internal_thought: I'm not confident I actually caught a repeat story—I was hosting the impression moment and wasn't paying close attention to every word. Better to tank my own 10 points than look like I'm making weak calls, and honestly it sets a good tone if the host admits when they're wrong.

jade.dialogue: Oh YES, Pat! I'm so here for this — okay okay, make your case, I'm listening! This is what game night is about!
jade.internal_thought: Pat's already killing it and now they're going for the big points? I'm voting yes no matter what because watching someone WIN is the actual dopamine hit.

river.dialogue: Yeah, I'll buy it. Pat's been talking since we sat down.
river.internal_thought: Pat's been going. I wasn't really tracking the story, but this feels right—they repeat stuff.

diana.dialogue: Okay, hold on—Pat, when did this happen? Because I've been tracking what everyone's said and I don't remember you telling a story yet. What are you claiming exactly?
diana.internal_thought: This is the third 'gotcha' claim in six minutes and I'm already tired of defending basic game integrity—if Pat's making this up just to test me, I'm going to lose it.

sam.dialogue: Oh, um... I think Pat might have a point? I wasn't totally following, but yeah, I'll listen to what they say.
sam.internal_thought: Wait, did Pat actually tell a story twice? I'm not even sure what story they're claiming about — I need to pay better attention but I don't want to look stupid by asking.

marcus.dialogue: Okay, Pat, what exactly did you hear? Because I was focused on my opening moves and I didn't catch it.
marcus.internal_thought: Another interruption. This is the third one in six minutes and we haven't even gotten to the actual game tension yet — but I need to actually listen to this one because Story Repeater is worth 10 points and if Pat's claiming it, I need to know if he's bullshitting or if I missed something.

tyler.dialogue: Wait, Pat's claiming what? Um... yeah, I wasn't really tracking that story.
tyler.internal_thought: Pat's been talking the whole time but I honestly wasn't listening closely enough to know if he repeated something or not.

votingMechanic.name: Crowd Cheer
votingMechanic.description: Rate it 1-5. Average above 3 and it passes.

sam.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.dialogue: Okay, wait—I'm calling bullshit on myself again. I wasn't even paying attention to when someone apologized, I was literally just refilling the chip bowl. This is the third claim in a row where I'm either making shit up or not actually tracking what I claimed. Can we pump the brakes for like thirty seconds?
pat.internal_thought: I've fumbled three claims in eight minutes and I'm clearly not in the mental space to be playing this AND hosting—Diana and Marcus are already checked out, and here I am looking sloppy with weak catches, which kills credibility and makes the game feel chaotic in a bad way instead of a good way.

jade.dialogue: Oh dude, YES — Pat's on a ROLL tonight! Okay I'm voting this a solid 5, that was totally sneaky and I love the energy!
jade.internal_thought: Pat's already got 15 points and is hunting hard — this is exactly the vibe I'm here for, everyone's getting spicy with it.

river.dialogue: Yeah, that checks out. I heard at least two 'sorrys' in the last round.
river.internal_thought: Pat's been genuinely apologizing for dumb stuff all night—easy call, and voting keeps things moving without drama.

diana.dialogue: Wait—Pat, when exactly did you hear someone say 'sorry'? Because I've been listening and I haven't caught that yet. Who said it and in what context?
diana.internal_thought: Pat's three for three with suspicious claims in eight minutes and I'm getting tired of being the only person asking basic questions about what actually happened.

marcus.dialogue: Okay, did Pat actually apologize, or are we just throwing points at anything now? Because I'm trying to focus on the opening here.
marcus.internal_thought: Pat's already got 15 points and these standing missions keep firing right when I'm trying to think through my strategy—this is exactly the chaos that derails focus.

alex.dialogue: Oh, Pat's gonna try that one? Honestly, I didn't even catch it — but let's see, did Pat actually apologize for something dumb or was it legitimate? I'm voting 4, this is going to be contested and I LIVE for the debate.
alex.internal_thought: Pat's playing the game now, which means more chaos is brewing — if this passes, we've got evidence Pat's claiming stuff; if it fails, Pat looks sloppy. Either way, the table's going to argue about what counts as 'no real reason' and I'm here for every second of it.

votingMechanic.name: Volunteer Tribunal
votingMechanic.description: Who wants to judge? First volunteers become the jury.

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

jade.dialogue: YES! Pat, you're absolutely ON FIRE right now — okay okay, I'm volunteering for the tribunal, let's hear it! This energy is UNHINGED and I am here for it!
jade.internal_thought: Pat's on a claiming rampage and honestly it's hilarious watching them hunt — I want front row seats to this tribunal, this is peak game night chaos.

river.dialogue: Yeah, I mean... I didn't really catch it, but Pat's been talking a lot. Could've happened.
river.internal_thought: Pat's racking up points fast and honestly I wasn't listening hard enough to actually know if they said 'um' — but voting yes keeps things moving and Pat's not worth the drama of calling bullshit.

diana.dialogue: Okay, hold on—Pat, I need specifics. WHO said 'um' and WHEN? Because you've made four claims in nine minutes and I've been listening carefully, and I need to know if this is actually grounded in something that happened or if we're just throwing darts at the board here.
diana.internal_thought: Pat is pattern-matching every standing mission to themselves and I'm not falling for it—they need to prove this actually happened, not just assume every speech quirk belongs to them.

sam.dialogue: Um... wait, did Pat actually say 'um' just now? I think so? I wasn't super focused on that one.
sam.internal_thought: Pat's claiming stuff every other minute and I have no idea if they're actually catching people or just making calls — this feels like maybe I should volunteer to judge but I don't want to mess it up or seem like I'm trying too hard on my first night.

marcus.dialogue: Are you KIDDING me right now? Pat's claiming every five seconds and we're STILL in setup. Can we actually play the game, or is this standing missions night?
marcus.internal_thought: This is the fourth claim in nine minutes and Pat's already got 20 points — I'm 0-3 and we haven't even started the real game yet, this is insane.

tyler.dialogue: Uh, wait — Pat caught someone saying 'um'? Okay yeah, sure, I'll... is someone judging this or...?
tyler.internal_thought: Pat's claiming something about 'um' — honestly I wasn't really tracking when that happened but it sounds legit, and I don't want to be the guy who doesn't volunteer for tribunal because that looks bad.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Diana is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

pat.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

river.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

sam.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

diana.dialogue: Wait—I need to be clear about exactly when and where I caught that. River mentioned 'Spielberg' when we were discussing the rulebook at minute 12, right after Sam asked about flash mission timing. That counts, yeah?
diana.internal_thought: Finally—a claim I can actually stand behind with evidence instead of just fishing, and it feels legit, but I'm NOT giving these vultures a reason to vote bullshit on me if I haven't got my facts perfectly lined up.

votingMechanic.name: Pitch It
votingMechanic.description: The claimant gets 15 seconds to make their case. Then you vote.

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.dialogue: Okay, Diana—pitch it. Who interrupted who? Because honestly, after that last stretch of claims, I'm actually curious if this one's legit.
pat.internal_thought: Diana's energy is steady and she's been quiet, so if she caught something real, that's actually good—she needs to feel engaged. But I'm still burned from three bogus claims in a row, so I'm listening skeptical. Let her make her case.

jade.dialogue: Oh YES, Diana! Okay okay, I'm so ready — lay it on us, what've you got? This is the good stuff right here!
jade.internal_thought: Diana's finally jumping in on the claims and I am HERE for it — everyone's getting their moment tonight, this is exactly what I came for.

river.dialogue: Yeah, I caught that—Sam cut off Tyler like thirty seconds ago when he was explaining the rules thing. Clean interrupt.
river.internal_thought: Diana's actually right, and I've been watching the whole time—this is exactly the kind of thing I should call out because I actually saw it.

diana.dialogue: Okay, so—two minutes ago, River literally cut off Sam mid-sentence about the scoring system to ask about drink refills. Clear interrupt. I've got the timestamp.
diana.internal_thought: Finally, a claim I can actually *defend* with specifics—and it's MY claim, so I'm not just poking holes in someone else's work.
diana.submission_if_applicable: River interrupted Sam at minute 30 when Sam was explaining the flash mission rules. River jumped in with 'Hey, who wants water?' — clear mid-sentence interrupt.

sam.dialogue: Oh, um... Diana, did you actually see someone interrupt? I think I missed it, but go ahead and explain what happened.
sam.internal_thought: Diana seems confident about this one, but I honestly don't remember if someone interrupted — I should just listen and let her pitch it before I vote, that's safer than looking stupid.

marcus.dialogue: Diana, what? Who interrupted who? Because I've been locked in on the actual game and I missed half of that. Can we just... play?
marcus.internal_thought: This is the fifth standing mission claim in nine minutes and we're barely through setup—Diana didn't even see an interruption, she's just fishing for points like everyone else.

alex.dialogue: Oh PERFECT timing, Diana! Okay okay, let's hear it—did you actually catch someone mid-sentence or are we about to watch this fall apart in real time? I'm so ready for this.
alex.internal_thought: Diana's been quiet for a bit, so either she caught something legit or she's about to make a terrible claim and I get to watch everyone tear her apart—either way, this is exactly the kind of moment that makes the night.

votingMechanic.name: Crowd Cheer
votingMechanic.description: Rate it 1-5. Average above 3 and it passes.

marcus.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.dialogue: Oh nice, Diana! Did you actually clock that? Let's hear it—who drank at the same time?
pat.internal_thought: Diana's been solid all night and this is actually a trackable claim unlike the last three disasters, so I'm genuinely curious if she caught something real.

jade.dialogue: OKAY DIANA, YES! Drink Spotter?! That's so good — I'm giving you a solid 5, that takes actual awareness, let's GO!
jade.internal_thought: This is exactly why I'm here — Diana's making moves, everyone's engaged, the chaos is flowing perfectly and I get to hype her up.

river.dialogue: Yeah, I saw that. She timed it right with Sam, like a minute ago.
river.internal_thought: Diana's actually been paying attention—I watched her catch that same moment I did, so she's not bullshitting.

diana.dialogue: Wait, wait, wait—I need to stop this right now. When exactly did I call this? Who was drinking at the same time as who? Because I've been listening to everyone the whole night and I don't remember actually SEEING that happen. Are we voting on something I claimed or something I'm supposed to have caught?
diana.internal_thought: Did I actually claim this or is the game system firing a standing mission and calling it MY claim? Because I have not been watching for synchronized drinks—I've been tracking every word people say for the other missions, and if I'm going to get 5 points I need to know exactly what I allegedly caught and when.

sam.dialogue: Oh! Yeah, I think I saw that — didn't Diana and like... someone else both reach for their drinks at basically the same time? That was pretty cool.
sam.internal_thought: Okay, this one I actually SAW happen, which feels good — I'm not just guessing or nodding along like an idiot.

tyler.dialogue: Oh, Diana got the drinks thing? Yeah, cool, I think I saw that... uh, what's the rating again?
tyler.internal_thought: Did Diana actually catch two people drinking at the same time or is she just claiming it happened? I wasn't really watching, but whatever, I'll throw out a number.

alex.dialogue: Oh, Diana's going for it! Okay okay, let's see if she actually caught it or if we're about to watch this implode — I'm giving it a 4, this is gonna be CONTESTED and I am HERE for it.
alex.internal_thought: Diana's been solid all night but Drink Spotter is actually hard to nail — did she really see two people at the exact same time or is she fishing? Either way this creates drama and I'm voting to keep the chaos rolling.

I KNOW someone swore, but I need to be absolutely certain before I put this in front of a jury, because if I'm wrong again, everyone's going to think I'm just gaming the system.

recommendations: Humor quality scored 3.2/10. Refresh the mission/poll prompt pools with more contextual humor.
Mini-games averaged 0.0/10 fun during n/a phase. Consider restricting mini-games to n/a phase.
Diana averaged 7.2/10 annoyance. Their persona type may need fewer/gentler events.
Only 25% would pay for AI features. The AI tier needs stronger differentiation from free static missions.
Screenshot-worthy moments scored 3.8/10. Add more visual/dramatic events (drawings, reveals).
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 88% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
Social media mention potential is low (2.5/10). Consider adding share-friendly moment recaps or highlight reels.
Group bonding scored 7.5/10 -- the social glue is working. Preserve collaborative events.

agentNarratives.name: Pat
agentNarratives.personality: Pat is the host and feels responsible for everyone's experience. They keep one eye on the snack table, one ear on the group energy, and will subtly steer the night if things get too intense or too dead. They vote strategically -- not to win, but to keep scores close and drama high. They'll sacrifice their own turn to resolve a dispute or refill drinks. Their goal isn't a personal win; it's a night everyone talks about later.

highlights.title: Celebrity Impression
highlights.dialogue: Oh, this is perfect—okay everyone, who's got the best one? I'm ready to be entertained. Jade, you look like you're about to explode, go ahead!

highlights.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
highlights.dialogue: Oh nice, Diana! Did you actually clock that? Let's hear it—who drank at the same time?

highlights.title: Claim: The Interrupter
highlights.dialogue: Okay, Diana—pitch it. Who interrupted who? Because honestly, after that last stretch of claims, I'm actually curious if this one's legit.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Apologizer
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, wait—I'm calling bullshit on myself again. I wasn't even paying attention to when someone apologized, I was literally just refilling the chip bowl. This is the third claim in a row where I'm either making shit up or not actually tracking what I claimed. Can we pump the brakes for like thirty seconds?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, hold on—I literally just caught someone checking their phone like thirty seconds ago? That's... actually weak timing on my part. Everyone, vote however you want, this one's on me.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, hold up—did I actually catch someone repeating a story? I wasn't even tracking that claim, I was focused on the impression thing. Let me think... no, I don't think that happened. I'm calling bullshit on my own claim here.

assessment.biggest_complaint: I was too distracted hosting to actually track my own claims, which made me feel like a fraud calling out observations I didn't really make.
assessment.best_moment: When Jade lit up doing the celebrity impression and everyone started riffing off each other—that's the kind of moment that gets remembered.
assessment.narrative_summary: Started rough because I was juggling hosting duties and couldn't focus on the claims properly, which honestly frustrated me. Once we hit the impressions round and Diana started nailing the 'Drink Spotter' call, the energy shifted and the night actually got fun. By the end, people were engaged and laughing, which is what matters—I just wish I'd been more present instead of splitting my attention between drinks and gameplay.
suggestions: Make claims easier to track or give hosts a 'pass' option so I'm not forced to make stuff up while managing the room
Space out the claims so there's actual downtime between calls—the back-to-back nature made it hard to stay honest
Maybe add a 'host exempt' mode where the person running the night can still play but without the pressure to catch everything
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Diana absolutely nailed this 'Drink Spotter' observation where she caught Marcus and Tyler both taking sips at the exact same moment, and when she called it out, Marcus nearly spit his drink everywhere trying to deny it—the chaos was perfect and it was totally legit.

agentNarratives.name: Jade
agentNarratives.personality: Jade is here for the people, not the points. She treats every game night like a reunion and every disruption like a party favor. She will abandon her turn mid-thought to hype someone else's joke, vote generously because she loves seeing people win, and volunteer for every flash mission just to see what happens. Winning is nice but watching everyone lose it laughing is better.

highlights.title: Claim: Um Counter
highlights.dialogue: YES! Pat, you're absolutely ON FIRE right now — okay okay, I'm volunteering for the tribunal, let's hear it! This energy is UNHINGED and I am here for it!

highlights.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
highlights.dialogue: OKAY DIANA, YES! Drink Spotter?! That's so good — I'm giving you a solid 5, that takes actual awareness, let's GO!

highlights.title: Claim: Story Repeater
highlights.dialogue: Oh YES, Pat! I'm so here for this — okay okay, make your case, I'm listening! This is what game night is about!

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Ooh, Pat's already hunting! Yeah, I'm voting that legit — gotta support the hustle early, let's get everyone fired up!

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh YES, Pat! I'm so here for this — okay okay, make your case, I'm listening! This is what game night is about!

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Apologizer
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh dude, YES — Pat's on a ROLL tonight! Okay I'm voting this a solid 5, that was totally sneaky and I love the energy!

assessment.biggest_complaint: Honestly? Nothing — 15 chaos events in 150 minutes kept things moving but never felt overwhelming.
assessment.best_moment: Pat on FIRE in the opening minutes with back-to-back claims while everyone's losing it trying to keep up with the energy.
assessment.narrative_summary: This was exactly what game night should be — pure energy and connection from start to finish. Pat started hot and kept the momentum going, Diana had that perfect Drink Spotter moment, and honestly I was too busy hyping people up and volunteering for everything to even think about my score. My energy literally went UP as the night went on because watching everyone light up beats winning any day.
suggestions: Keep the flash mission rate exactly where it is — the single flash kept things spicy without overwhelming
Maybe throw in a few more opportunities for group hyping moments like tribunals
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat claiming 'The Apologizer' and being SO sneaky about it while everyone's just nodding along until it clicks — the whole table just erupted and that's when I knew this night was going to be legendary.

agentNarratives.name: River
agentNarratives.personality: River sits back, observes, and picks their moments carefully. They rarely initiate conversation but give surprisingly sharp answers when asked directly. They'll skip most flash missions but nail a standing mission out of nowhere because they've been quietly watching the whole time. They vote generously because they don't care enough to argue, but their mini-game submissions are secretly devastating. You forget River is there until they drop a one-liner that floors the room.

highlights.title: Claim: The Interrupter
highlights.dialogue: Yeah, I caught that—Sam cut off Tyler like thirty seconds ago when he was explaining the rules thing. Clean interrupt.

highlights.title: Claim: Story Repeater
highlights.dialogue: Yeah, I'll buy it. Pat's been talking since we sat down.

highlights.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
highlights.dialogue: Yeah, I saw that. She timed it right with Sam, like a minute ago.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Legit. Pretty sure I saw that.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: Yeah, I'll buy it. Pat's been talking since we sat down.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Felt like I was just validating observations everyone else already noticed—there wasn't much to actually play.
assessment.best_moment: When I caught Sam cutting Tyler off at minute 32 and called it out cleanly; that one landed.
assessment.narrative_summary: Spent most of the night watching the room, which is fine, but the chaos agent turned into me basically being a silent jury rubber-stamping things people were already aware of. My energy actually went up by the end because I started checking out mentally. It wasn't bad, just kind of hollow—like being asked to confirm what you already know.
suggestions: Make events require actual deduction or hidden information instead of just asking people to verify what they already saw
Drop the frequency—15 events in 150 minutes meant constant low-level interruption instead of punctuated chaos
Give sitting-back players like me something to actually do besides nod along
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Nothing really stands out—it was all pretty low-key observation-based stuff that doesn't translate well to someone who wasn't watching the whole night anyway.

agentNarratives.name: Diana
agentNarratives.personality: Diana reads the rulebook before anyone else touches it and will pause the game to settle an edge case. She doesn't hate chaos -- she hates unfair chaos. If a flash mission feels rigged or a voting mechanic seems arbitrary, she'll call it out loudly and demand clarification. She's competitive in a principled way: she wants to win, but only if the win was earned under proper conditions. Other players either love her precision or dread her "actually..." moments.

highlights.title: Claim: The Interrupter
highlights.dialogue: Okay, so—two minutes ago, River literally cut off Sam mid-sentence about the scoring system to ask about drink refills. Clear interrupt. I've got the timestamp.

highlights.title: Claim: Name Dropper
highlights.dialogue: Wait—I need to be clear about exactly when and where I caught that. River mentioned 'Spielberg' when we were discussing the rulebook at minute 12, right after Sam asked about flash mission timing. That counts, yeah?

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Hold on—what exactly did you see? Because 'Phone Addict' requires someone actually checking their phone when they should be paying attention, and we're literally three minutes into setup. When was this supposed to happen?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Um Counter
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, hold on—Pat, I need specifics. WHO said 'um' and WHEN? Because you've made four claims in nine minutes and I've been listening carefully, and I need to know if this is actually grounded in something that happened or if we're just throwing darts at the board here.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, wait, wait—I need to stop this right now. When exactly did I call this? Who was drinking at the same time as who? Because I've been listening to everyone the whole night and I don't remember actually SEEING that happen. Are we voting on something I claimed or something I'm supposed to have caught?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Potty Mouth
lowPoints.dialogue: Hold on—I need to stop this. I'm claiming 'Potty Mouth' but I need to be crystal clear about what I actually caught. Who swore, when, and in what exact context, because I've been paying attention all night and I want to make sure this claim is airtight before we even get to a tribunal.

assessment.biggest_complaint: The system doesn't adequately distinguish between a claim you actually witnessed and a claim someone else made that you're voting on, which means the entire verification framework falls apart when people stop being precise.
assessment.best_moment: Catching River's interrupt at minute 32 with a timestamp—that was clean, that was verifiable, that was the game working exactly as intended.
assessment.narrative_summary: I came in hot because the rulebook felt underspecified on claim verification, and that set the tone for the whole night. I won first place, which felt good, but I spent about a third of the evening pumping the brakes on Pat and others making wild claims without proper grounding. The game rewarded my attention to detail, but it also made me the person everyone knew was going to demand receipts. By the end I was genuinely energized—not because the chaos was fun, but because I'd successfully held the line on standards.
suggestions: Before the game starts, make it crystal clear: a CLAIM requires the claimer to have witnessed something AND be able to cite specific timing/context. If someone can't back it up in real-time, it doesn't go to tribunal.
Add a 30-second 'claim verification window' where the claimer has to provide at least one other player who also saw/heard the thing, or it's automatically challenged.
Flash missions feel too easy to game—consider requiring a 2-player witness minimum before awarding points, because right now one person's memory = one person's points.
The Phone Addict claim at minute 3 (literally during setup) shouldn't be possible. Define clear windows for when each claim type can activate.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat making four claims in nine minutes with zero specifics, and me having to actually pull out my phone and start time-stamping everything like I was gathering evidence for a trial. The table's face when I said 'WHO said um and WHEN' was incredible—they knew they were dealing with someone who wasn't letting chaos hide sloppy thinking.

agentNarratives.name: Sam
agentNarratives.personality: Sam is at their first game night with this group and still figuring out the vibe. They laugh at jokes a beat too late, hesitate before voting, and get visibly overwhelmed when multiple events fire in quick succession. After three or more events within ten minutes their engagement drops sharply -- they'll go quiet, stop checking the app, and just watch until the pace calms down. They want to belong but don't want to look foolish trying.

highlights.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
highlights.dialogue: Oh! Yeah, I think I saw that — didn't Diana and like... someone else both reach for their drinks at basically the same time? That was pretty cool.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Um... did Pat actually catch someone on their phone? I wasn't really paying attention to that.

highlights.title: Claim: The Interrupter
highlights.dialogue: Oh, um... Diana, did you actually see someone interrupt? I think I missed it, but go ahead and explain what happened.

lowPoints.title: Celebrity Impression
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh... um... I'm not really great at impressions. Maybe I'll just watch this one.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, um... I think Pat might have a point? I wasn't totally following, but yeah, I'll listen to what they say.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Um Counter
lowPoints.dialogue: Um... wait, did Pat actually say 'um' just now? I think so? I wasn't super focused on that one.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Things happened way too fast at the beginning and I totally lost track of what was going on, which made me feel kind of left out.
assessment.best_moment: When I actually spotted Diana reaching for her drink at the same time as someone else — felt like I finally caught onto what was happening.
assessment.narrative_summary: My first game night with this group was... okay? I had a rough start because there were so many things happening at once and I couldn't keep up, which honestly made me want to just sit back and watch for a bit. But once things slowed down, I got more into it and even caught a couple of things the app was looking for, which felt pretty good. By the end my energy was way up, but I'm not totally sure the chaos agent thing is necessary — the actual dinner party and people were fun enough on their own.
suggestions: Maybe space out the events more at the beginning so new people like me don't feel so overwhelmed right away
It might help to explain what people are supposed to be watching for before things start happening
Some of the observations felt like they needed way more focus than I could give while also talking and eating
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Diana and someone else both grabbed their drinks at literally the exact same second — the timing was so perfectly synchronized that it had to be intentional, but everyone acted like it was just a coincidence.

agentNarratives.name: Marcus
agentNarratives.personality: Marcus plays to win. He studies strategy guides before game night and tracks his win rate across sessions. Interruptions during critical game moments genuinely irritate him, though he'll tolerate lighter chaos during downtime. He respects clever plays but has zero patience for random disruptions.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Wait, who was even on their phone? I wasn't paying attention to Pat's phone, I was setting up my strategy. This feels like a reach.

highlights.title: Claim: Story Repeater
highlights.dialogue: Okay, Pat, what exactly did you hear? Because I was focused on my opening moves and I didn't catch it.

highlights.title: Claim: The Apologizer
highlights.dialogue: Okay, did Pat actually apologize, or are we just throwing points at anything now? Because I'm trying to focus on the opening here.

lowPoints.title: Celebrity Impression
lowPoints.dialogue: Come on, we're five minutes in. Can we please just play the actual game instead of doing impressions every two minutes?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Um Counter
lowPoints.dialogue: Are you KIDDING me right now? Pat's claiming every five seconds and we're STILL in setup. Can we actually play the game, or is this standing missions night?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Potty Mouth
lowPoints.dialogue: Are you serious right now? Diana, I wasn't even paying attention to who said what — I'm trying to figure out my next three moves and you're asking me to be a judge on whether someone cursed? This is exactly what I'm talking about.

assessment.biggest_complaint: The chaos agent turned a strategy game into a constant judgment exercise where I couldn't focus on actual gameplay for more than a few minutes before being interrupted.
assessment.best_moment: Honestly, I'm not sure there was one — maybe the brief window at the start before the interruptions ramped up.
assessment.narrative_summary: I came in ready to play strategically and track my performance, but the dinner party got hijacked by constant claims and judgments that had nothing to do with the actual game. By minute 9 I was already frustrated, and it never got better. I finished 4th out of 8, which is exactly what happens when you can't concentrate on your strategy because you're being asked every 30 seconds to judge whether someone said 'um' or repeated a story.
suggestions: If you're going to add interruptions, make them actual game mechanics tied to scoring, not random observer judgments
Respect the first 15 minutes as sacred setup time — no claims, no interruptions
Cap the total chaos events at 5-6 per 150 minutes, not 15
Only trigger events that are objective and verifiable, not subjective social observations
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Not really funny, but at minute 56 Diana asked me to judge whether someone cursed while I was literally in the middle of planning my next three moves — I had to remind her that I wasn't paying attention to casual conversation, I was trying to win.

agentNarratives.name: Tyler
agentNarratives.personality: Tyler is physically present but mentally on his phone about 40% of the time. He misses flash missions entirely, asks "wait, what happened?" after key moments, and votes randomly because he wasn't paying attention to the claim. He's not malicious -- he genuinely wants to be here -- but his attention drifts to texts, TikToks, and fantasy league updates constantly. When he does engage, it's usually 10 seconds too late.

highlights.title: Claim: Story Repeater
highlights.dialogue: Wait, Pat's claiming what? Um... yeah, I wasn't really tracking that story.

highlights.title: Claim: Um Counter
highlights.dialogue: Uh, wait — Pat caught someone saying 'um'? Okay yeah, sure, I'll... is someone judging this or...?

highlights.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
highlights.dialogue: Oh, Diana got the drinks thing? Yeah, cool, I think I saw that... uh, what's the rating again?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Um Counter
lowPoints.dialogue: Uh, wait — Pat caught someone saying 'um'? Okay yeah, sure, I'll... is someone judging this or...?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, Pat's claiming what? Um... yeah, I wasn't really tracking that story.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, Diana got the drinks thing? Yeah, cool, I think I saw that... uh, what's the rating again?

assessment.biggest_complaint: I missed like half the stuff that was happening and had to keep asking people to catch me up.
assessment.best_moment: When Diana nailed the Drink Spotter thing and everyone reacted, that was pretty solid.
assessment.narrative_summary: Honestly, I was only half-here tonight because my phone kept going off with notifications. The game itself was fine — there were these chaos events happening but I kind of tuned in and out. I caught a few moments where people were making claims about stuff, but I missed the details. By the end I was way more energized than when we started, so that's good. It wasn't bad, just felt like I was always a step behind.
suggestions: Maybe announce events louder so people like me actually notice them
Make it clearer what's being judged and how — I was confused half the time
Consider shorter events since some of us have shorter attention spans
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat trying to call out that story thing while I was literally staring at my phone, and then asking me to vote and I just said 'sure' without even knowing what was happening.

agentNarratives.name: Alex
agentNarratives.personality: Alex lives for chaos and treats every game mechanic as a system to exploit. They send signals constantly, claim missions they barely completed just to see if the group will call bullshit, and deliberately provoke flash missions at the worst possible moments. They vote strategically -- always picking the option that creates the most drama. Alex doesn't care about winning; they care about creating the story everyone retells for months. If there's a loophole, Alex has already found it and is halfway through abusing it.

highlights.title: Celebrity Impression
highlights.dialogue: Oh, THIS is what I'm talking about! *immediately stands up* Okay, okay, picture this—

highlights.title: Claim: The Apologizer
highlights.dialogue: Oh, Pat's gonna try that one? Honestly, I didn't even catch it — but let's see, did Pat actually apologize for something dumb or was it legitimate? I'm voting 4, this is going to be contested and I LIVE for the debate.

highlights.title: Claim: The Interrupter
highlights.dialogue: Oh PERFECT timing, Diana! Okay okay, let's hear it—did you actually catch someone mid-sentence or are we about to watch this fall apart in real time? I'm so ready for this.

lowPoints.title: Celebrity Impression
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, THIS is what I'm talking about! *immediately stands up* Okay, okay, picture this—

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Apologizer
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, Pat's gonna try that one? Honestly, I didn't even catch it — but let's see, did Pat actually apologize for something dumb or was it legitimate? I'm voting 4, this is going to be contested and I LIVE for the debate.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Interrupter
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh PERFECT timing, Diana! Okay okay, let's hear it—did you actually catch someone mid-sentence or are we about to watch this fall apart in real time? I'm so ready for this.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Only one flash mission all night — I needed more opportunities to push people into impossible situations.
assessment.best_moment: Pat's claim got absolutely demolished when Diana called out the contradiction, and I watched the whole table descend into five minutes of chaos debating whether the apologizer even counted.
assessment.narrative_summary: This was exactly the kind of night I live for — zero downtime, constant pressure on people to defend their claims, and enough gray area in the rules to drive everyone insane. I came in hot and never let the energy drop. By the end my adrenaline was maxed out because every single event was a potential flashpoint for argument, and that's the game I'm actually playing.
suggestions: Add more contested-territory events where the rules are deliberately ambiguous so players can argue about what counts
Let chaos agents trigger flash missions on demand once per round — I want to be able to punish specific moments
Make claims worth more points if contested and upheld; right now playing it safe is too rewarded
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: When Diana claimed she interrupted someone and Pat immediately said 'You were literally listening to your phone the whole time,' and then the table split 50/50 on whether phone scrolling counts as the event happening — I voted for the most controversial answer just to watch Pat's face.

recommendations: Humor quality scored 3.2/10. Refresh the mission/poll prompt pools with more contextual humor.
Mini-games averaged 0.0/10 fun during n/a phase. Consider restricting mini-games to n/a phase.
Diana averaged 7.2/10 annoyance. Their persona type may need fewer/gentler events.
Only 25% would pay for AI features. The AI tier needs stronger differentiation from free static missions.
Screenshot-worthy moments scored 3.8/10. Add more visual/dramatic events (drawings, reveals).
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 88% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
Social media mention potential is low (2.5/10). Consider adding share-friendly moment recaps or highlight reels.
Group bonding scored 7.5/10 -- the social glue is working. Preserve collaborative events.
